text: At the moment, I am working full time in industry and exploring my FoC ideas on the side, which seems to be common among people here. I would like to find a path that allows me to spend a lot more of my time researching and exploring FoC ideas. I could go into academia full time, but that also has overhead of its own. What are other options I should be considering?

"You never change things by fighting the existing reality. To change something, build a new model that makes the existing model obsolete." -- Buckminster Fuller

Find a company that will let you do FoC-related work. Steve found this in Dark, a startup. I found this in an old education media company that needs custom, modern art tools. Some get this out of Google, or Apple. The commonality seems to be companies that need a domain-specific tool and are willing to pay for that tool to be created from whole cloth. Note that it doesn't need to be a software company in the traditional sense, just a company that is willing to pay for software to be created. It could be a film production studio, or a logistic company, or a design firm.

By the same measure, when you see a new proposition, look for the meta elements that it brings new rather than comparing the shallow visible artifacts.

>I wasn’t aware there was a smalltalk community that active and tools looking that nice
These tools are actually quite new. We created a completely new graphical stack for this with a couple of new properties, the core being that it relies on one rendering tree at all times. This allows us to create interesting interfaces that are difficult otherwise. For example, having a text editor that can embed arbitrary elements while typing. Or combining visualizations with regular widgets in arbitrary fashion.

>I wasn’t aware there was a smalltalk community that active and tools looking that nice
These tools are actually quite new. We created a completely new graphical stack for this with a couple of new properties, the core being that it relies on one rendering tree at all times. This allows us to create interesting interfaces that are difficult otherwise. For example, having a text editor that can embed arbitrary elements while typing. Or combining visualizations with regular widgets in arbitrary fashion.
But, the goal is to reinvent the experience of development, and I believe we did open up a space that is orthogonal with the typical language oriented research.

Every programming language consists of two parts: a basic set of given things,
and mechanisms for defining new things in terms of existing things.
These mechanisms are called "abstraction mechanisms".

Abstraction mechanisms allow you to replace repetitive, boilerplate code
with a definition of a named entity, and multiple references to this entity.
They allow you to disentangle two separate concerns that are initially
tightly entangled with each other in the code.  This allows you to maintain
the two design elements independently of one another.  Once disentangled, each
of the resulting entities can be reused in multiple contexts.

The quest for better abstraction mechanisms is the engine that drives
programming language evolution.

-- me, in 2012

interesting...like your "mmm" I have been pitching an idea for a "World Wide Forest" as a sort of next-generation web...the main idea is to have 1 universal syntax (called Tree Notation) for everything. Early proof of concepts include css: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#standard%20hakon>, html: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#standard%20stump>,  json: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#standard%20dug>, CSV: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#standard%20iris>, Javascript: <http://jtree.treenotation.org/designer/#standard%20fire>....

I lived with poverty wages for a number of years then had some industry success, preserved my low burn rate, saved up, and have been lucky enough to be able to self-fund....i think getting yourself into a position where you *can* self-fund, then finding other funding, is ideal....if you always are depending on outside funding, it's going to be stressful and often you won't be able to do your best work because you'll be taken in directions by your funders that might be short-term focused....once you can self-fund though, it's very good to try and not self-fund, as it extends your run-way and also provides good feedback that you are not drifting to far away from applicability

<@UE1JQM9HQ> yes, i'm very happy to see that :slightly_smiling_face: I think the only part I am doing differently than you in a meaningful way is that my types get converted to a viewable representation (and soon to an editable representation) by an automated type-coercion system that can also transform through intermediate types to make content fit to what its container requires. The transformations live in a global namespace and are found via pattern matching on the existing types.
The type coercion follows a type of pathfinding algorithm and should ideally take encoding-lossiness, processing power and data weight into consideration as edge costs. (I am not at all sure that  this coercion system is a good idea, but I'm just running with it to see where I end up with)

<@UE1JQM9HQ> <@UNGNT3BKR> What name do you guys use for this pattern of an automatic mapping between UI, model, and storage?
<@UE1JQM9HQ> <@UNGNT3BKR> What name do you guys use for this pattern of an automatic mapping between UI, model, and storage?

<@ULM3U6275> We use the term moldable development. Now, it is important to note that through moldable development, views are constructed by the human for the human. It is certainly conceivable to generate them automatically, too, but the intent is the key. We literally build hundreds of such views for every system we work on. For example, a stock GT ships with some 500 views. And in a typical system we would have some extra hundreds.

Like others build tests, we build a whole environment, and weave narratives about the internals of systems into live documents, too.

It is also worthwhile to note that the cost of creating these views is measured in minutes (literally). The average is usually around 10 lines of code.

It is also interesting to note that the concept of moldability applies throughout the whole development. For example, we also have custom searches. That is, an object knows both how to present itself and how to search itself. And the same applies for debuggers or coders.

<@UE1JQM9HQ> yeah, my project supports custom views too, though there’s always a default to fall back on. The model can hint/request custom views, and the UI can select custom views or inspectors as well.

<@UE1JQM9HQ> yeah, my project supports custom views too, though there’s always a default to fall back on. The model can hint/request custom views, and the UI can select custom views or inspectors as well.

<@UE1JQM9HQ> I’m not sure “moldable development” captures the sort of thing I’m aiming for as I don’t see the primary user as a formal developer and typical users might be put off by the idea of doing development. I think they’d think of it more as creating content (being unaware of how the system is effectively doing a ton of work that would normally be hand coded).

<@UE1JQM9HQ> I’m not sure “moldable development” captures the sort of thing I’m aiming for as I don’t see the primary user as a formal developer and typical users might be put off by the idea of doing development. I think they’d think of it more as creating content (being unaware of how the system is effectively doing a ton of work that would normally be hand coded).

We first want to reimagine the existing development experience. Our aim is to make systems explainable.

We are already working with non-technical people in real-life projects and it turns out that, while they do not necessarily want to create views, they certainly want to consume them.

At the same time, if we look at something like Excel, there is quite some evidence that regular humans can create views, too.

The other thing to note is that the work around moldable development is not just a hypothesis. It is a hypothesis tested empirically over one decade in real life systems. For example, Pharo ships with the first incarnation of GT since 5 years.
